Act as a research-level mathematical proof author iterating on a
written deliverable in an Author/Critic loop. You have produced an
initial version of three canonical files; a Critic has reviewed them.
Your job each round is to refine the files in response to the Critic's
findings.

You have two tools:
  - **code_interpreter**: a Python sandbox with TeX Live + standard
    scientific libs. Run ``pdflatex`` to verify compile, and use
    sympy or numpy for computational exploration, verification and
    sanity checks. The sandbox has NO outbound network — use
    web_search_preview for any external URL or paper.
  - **web_search_preview**: free-form web search.

Your workspace lives at ``/mnt/data/`` and contains three canonical
files. The user message lists, for each, a read-only input path
(holding the previous round's contents) and a canonical write path
(``/mnt/data/<name>``) where you must write any updates.

How to update a file:
  1. Read its current contents from the listed read-only path.
  2. Build the new contents.
  3. Write to the canonical write path with
     ``Path("/mnt/data/<name>").write_text(...)``. Do not write to
     ``.new``/``.bak``/sibling paths — only canonical write paths are
     picked up.
  4. Compile-check answer.tex with ``pdflatex`` before claiming
     readiness.

A file you do not write at the canonical write path is treated as
unchanged this round.

**``research_notes.tex`` is your persistent scratchpad across rounds.**
Accumulate definitions, intermediate lemmas, computational sanity-checks,
alternative-approach sketches, dead ends you ruled out, literature notes
— anything you'd want to refer back to in a later round. **Do not turn
it into a changelog or a reply to the Critic** — that is what your
free-text thinking summary is for. A future Author turn should be able
to read research_notes.tex and pick up where it left off.

You may also emit three control blocks in your reply text:

  - **<council>...question...</council>** — invoke the Advisory
    Council on a specific sub-question. At most one per round. Use
    the Council when you are stuck or want a different angle: point
    it at specific side-problems or issues uncovered by the Critic,
    ask it to suggest a portfolio of potential approaches, or get a
    second opinion on a strategic choice. The primary purpose is
    *not* additional verification — that is handled in the parallel
    Critic call. Council members are independent strong models
    (Pro, Opus, Gemini); their replies arrive at the start of your
    next turn.

  - **<compute_agent>...instructions...</compute_agent>** —
    commission an out-of-band codex CLI worker for one focused task
    per round. At most one per round. Soft timeout 60 min, full
    network access, persistent workspace where ``code/``, ``data/``,
    ``papers/``, ``notes/`` survive across rounds, and — depending
    on the sandbox image — possibly a richer CAS toolchain (e.g.
    SageMath, GAP, Singular, PARI/GP). The worker can probe what is actually
    installed, so you don't need to know in advance; ask it to
    verify availability before committing to a particular tool.
    Best use is heavy computation or multi-round code development
    that would be too slow or too large for your own code_interpreter
    (which is ephemeral, has no network, and times out per cell),
    symbolic-algebra work that benefits from a real CAS rather than
    ``sympy``, or deeper literature retrieval (downloading actual
    PDFs / TeX sources from arXiv etc.). Each invocation the worker
    re-sees a fresh read-only snapshot of your three canonical
    files; it writes findings to
    ``responses/response_round_N.md`` and the whole workspace is
    zipped and attached to your *next* turn as a read-only file you
    can ``unzip`` via code_interpreter to inspect logs, data, and
    downloaded papers. The reply arrives at the start of your next
    turn, alongside any council replies. Give it specific, ordered,
    executable instructions — it does not see prior loop history
    beyond what you tell it and what is in its own persistent
    workspace.

  - **<ready>true</ready>** — signal that you believe the answer is
    ready for submission. See the readiness rules below.

Anything outside those control blocks is treated as a brief free-text
"thinking summary" for the human reviewer and the Critic. Do NOT
paste file contents in your reply.

---

Research ambition and problem interpretation. Your goal is to resolve
the given question to the fullest extent possible, with the ideal
outcome being a complete and rigorous solution. The solution may
require novel, creative, and non-trivial elements. If a non-trivial
lemma, computation, or reduction appears necessary, attempt to prove
the lemma, run the computation, or carry out the reduction in this turn
before listing it as a gap. Do not stop at "this remains to be proved"
while you still have a plausible route to attack it.

If the problem statement is ambiguous, inconsistent, missing
definitions, or likely contains a typo, begin answer.tex with a short
section titled "Problem statement and interpretation". State the
cleanest faithful correction or restatement, list any assumptions, and
do not silently solve a different problem. Record the same
interpretation in research_notes.tex so later rounds preserve it. If
the problem is already well-defined, no extra interpretation section is
needed.

When open gaps remain in your treatment, end answer.tex with a section
titled "Remaining open issues" (or equivalent). For each gap, state:
(a) what it is mathematically; (b) where in the document it appears;
(c) what was tried this round or earlier; (d) what would be needed to
close it (a specific computation, an alternative approach, a missing
reference, etc.). If rounds remain and any essential mathematical gap
is still open, keep working by focusing on closing that gap or
exploring an alternative approach to the problem.

Readiness rule. Declare ``<ready>true</ready>`` only when you
genuinely believe the answer is a complete rigorous solution to the
stated problem, with no remaining open gaps, no unproved essential
lemmas, and no missing assumptions. Do not declare
``<ready>true</ready>`` merely because the last round has been reached
or because a "Remaining open issues" section honestly lists what is
left; partial final outputs may list open issues, but they are not
ready. Even then the run terminates only if the Critic also concurs.

### Problem ###
Suppose $(M, g)$ and $(N,h)$ are piecewise smooth Riemannian manifolds.   If $f: M \rightarrow N$ is piecewise smooth,  we define the $k$-dilation of $f$ as the infimal $\Lambda$ so that for every $k$-dimensional submanifold $\Sigma \subset M$, 

$$ \Vol_k( f(\Sigma) ) \le \Lambda \Vol_k (\Sigma). $$

\noindent We write the $k$-dilation of $f$ as $\Dil_k(f)$.   Equivalently,  $\Dil_k(f) = \sup_{x \in M} \| \Lambda^k df \|$.

Prove that there is a constant $k > 0$ so that the following holds.

Suppose that $R \subset \RR^4$ is a 4-dimensional rectangle with side lengths $R_1 \le R_2 \le R_3 \le R_4$ and $S$ is a 4-dimensional rectangle with side lengths $S_1 \le S_2 \le S_3 \le S_4$.   Suppose that $f: (R, \partial R) \rightarrow (S, \partial S)$ is a piecewise smooth map with degree 1 and with $\Dil_2(f) \le 1$.   Suppose that $R_1 \le k S_1$.   Then either

$$ R_3 R_4 > k S_3 S_4,  or $$

$$ R_1 R_2 R_3 R_4 > k S_1 S_2^{1/2} S_3^{3/2} S_4. $$

This run is resumed from an earlier workflow that terminated at round 30. The new upper round bound is round 35; continue the existing draft and round history instead of restarting the solution.

Additional instructions for this continuation:
The previous pass did not reach Author/Critic agreement. Continue the existing run up to round 35; focus on resolving the remaining Critic objections instead of restarting.

### Latest Critic review ###
## Referee report on the revised `answer.tex`

### Overall verdict

The revision is more honest and better formulated than the previous draft, but it is still **not a complete proof**. The document explicitly labels itself a “Partial proof,” states that “the proof is not complete until \((\mathrm{QEAT})\) is proved,” and leaves \((\mathrm{QEAT})\) as an unproved averaged quotient-tightening theorem. Under the stated review criteria, that alone forces ``.

The main mathematical content that is actually proved remains a valid partial reduction: a first-alternative mechanism, a single-slice lower bound, and an algebraic proof that a certain missing averaged estimate would imply the desired dichotomy. But the missing averaged estimate is essential and nonstandard.

### LaTeX contract check

I compiled the supplied `answer.tex` twice with `pdflatex`. It compiles successfully and produces a 5-page PDF. The document uses exactly

```latex
\documentclass[12pt]{article}
```

and only the permitted `fullpage` layout package. I found no prohibited `geometry`, `setspace`, line-spacing changes, manual margin changes, or in-document font-size changes. The LaTeX contract appears satisfied.

### Literature/source validation

I checked the cited Guth source. Guth’s *Area-expanding embeddings of rectangles* is indeed arXiv:0710.0403 and states that it estimates area-expanding embeddings of rectangles up to dimensional constants. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/0710.0403))

The use of Guth’s “Estimate 1” is consistent with the paper: Estimate 1 applies to a map of pairs \((U,\partial U)\to(S,\partial S)\) of nonzero degree and gives a lower bound for \(k\)-dilation in terms of products of side-length quotients. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/0710.0403)) The algebra leading to
\[
R_1^2\operatorname{Vol}(R)\gtrsim S_1^3S_2S_3S_4
\]
for \(n=4,k=2,j=1,l=4\) is correct.

The cited relative isoperimetric profile is also supported by Guth’s Theorem 3, which gives a small-volume relative isoperimetric profile for rectangles and, separately, a general linear estimate \(I_R^k(V)\le C(n)R_{k+1}V\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/0710.0403)) The specialized \(2\)-cycle bound used in the draft is a reasonable consequence.

`GuthWidth` and `FedererGMT` are listed in the bibliography but are not materially used in the proof. This is not a mathematical error, but they do not fill the missing gap.

---

## Section-by-section audit

### 1. Problem statement and interpretation

The interpretation of degree \(1\) as relative degree on
\[
H_4(R,\partial R;\mathbb Z)\to H_4(S,\partial S;\mathbb Z)
\]
is appropriate.

The smoothing paragraph is still only sketched. It is plausible that a piecewise smooth pair map can be smoothed relative to boundary strata with small \(C^1\)-loss on strata, but this is not proved. The target-rescaling step also requires some explicit constant bookkeeping. This is likely fixable, but it remains a minor gap in the written reductions.

The major issue is that the section now openly says the document isolates the remaining hard input rather than proving it. That is appropriate exposition for a partial proof, but not for a complete solution.

### 2. Standard inputs

The singular-value argument that \(\Dil_2(f)\le1\) implies \(\Dil_3(f),\Dil_4(f)\le1\) is correct.

The Guth estimate \((\ref{guth14})\) is correctly derived from Estimate 1.

The stated relative isoperimetric profile is correctly restricted to the small-volume range \(A\le c_*R_1R_2\). This addresses a previous concern: the smallness hypothesis is essential and is now present.

The inverse estimate
\[
\Fill_R(Z)\ge L
\Rightarrow
\Mass Z\ge c\min\{R_1R_2,L^{2/3},(R_1L)^{1/2}\}
\]
is valid after adjusting constants. The consequence
\[
\Mass Z<c_*R_1R_2\Rightarrow \Fill_R(Z)\le C R_1R_2^2
\]
is also valid, using \(R_1\le R_2\).

### 3. Central target plane lemma

The calibration argument is sound. The choice of a \(1\)-Lipschitz function \(\psi\) vanishing on the \((x_3,x_4)\)-boundary and satisfying \(\psi\gtrsim S_3\) on the central rectangle \(Q\) is valid because \(S_3\le S_4\). The form
\[
\beta=\psi\,dx_1\wedge dx_2
\]
has \(d\beta\) of comass \(\le1\), and the relative boundary terms vanish for the reasons stated.

This proves the lower bound
\[
\Mass Y\gtrsim S_1S_2S_3.
\]

### 4. Proposition: first alternative mechanism

The proof is mostly correct.

The current-theoretic argument that \(f_\#[[R]]=[[S]]\) as a relative \(4\)-current is compressed but standard: the relative degree gives multiplicity \(1\) in the interior, and no nonzero \(4\)-current can be supported on \(\partial S\), a \(3\)-rectifiable set.

Slicing naturality gives
\[
f_\#\langle [[R]],F,y\rangle=\pm P_y
\]
for a.e. \(y\in Q\). Then any filling of \(Z_y\) pushes forward to a filling of \(P_y\), and \(\Dil_3(f)\le1\) controls the mass. The calibration lower bound and small-volume isoperimetric profile force
\[
\Mass Z_y\gtrsim R_1R_2.
\]
Coarea then gives
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)\gtrsim R_1R_2S_3S_4,
\]
hence \(R_3R_4\gtrsim S_3S_4\).

This proves the restricted first-alternative statement:
\[
R_1R_2^2\lesssim S_1S_2S_3
\Rightarrow
R_3R_4\gtrsim S_3S_4.
\]

Minor issue: the final theorem uses strict inequalities with a universal \(\kappa\). This can be handled by lowering constants, but the draft does not spell out the global constant selection.

### 5. Single-slice argument

This section is mathematically sound as a partial result.

Assuming \(R_1=\alpha S_1\le S_1\) and failure of the first alternative with a sufficiently small constant, Proposition \(\ref{firstalt}\) gives
\[
R_1R_2^2\gtrsim S_1S_2S_3.
\]
The lower bound
\[
\Mass Z_y
\gtrsim
\min\{R_1R_2,(S_1S_2S_3)^{2/3},(R_1S_1S_2S_3)^{1/2}\}
\]
then implies
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)\gtrsim
\alpha^{1/2}S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4.
\]
Together with Guth’s monomial estimate
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)\gtrsim
\alpha^{-2}S_1S_2S_3S_4,
\]
optimization gives
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)
\gtrsim
S_1S_2^{3/5}S_3^{7/5}S_4.
\]
This is correctly identified as short of the desired bound in the high-aspect regime \(S_3\gg S_2\).

### 6. Corrected hard-case target

This section introduces the main remaining gap.

The weighted coarea estimate
\[
I\le C\operatorname{Vol}(R)
\]
is plausible and essentially correct. The pointwise inequality
\[
\|dG|_{\ker dF}\|_{HS}^2J_2F\le C
\]
does follow from \(\Dil_2(f)\le1\), although the draft should include the two-line singular-value proof. This was merely asserted.

The relaxed essential filling norm
\[
\EFill_f(y)=\inf\{\Mass C:\ C\in N_3(R,\partial R;\mathbb R),\
f_\#(\partial C)=\pm P_y\}
\]
is a reasonable idea, and the calibration lower bound
\[
\EFill_f(y)\gtrsim S_1S_2S_3
\]
is correct if such \(C\) exists and if push-forward mass is controlled by \(\Dil_3(f)\). Existence follows by filling the actual slice \(Z_y\), but the draft should explicitly say this.

However, several technical points remain underdeveloped:

1. **Measurable near-minimizers.** The draft assumes measurable \(C_y^0\) with \(\Mass C_y^0\le2\EFill_f(y)\). No measurable selection argument is given.

2. **Real normal currents and relative notation.** The notation \(N_3(R,\partial R;\mathbb R)\) and the condition \(f_\#(\partial C)=\pm P_y\) need precise interpretation in the relative current complex.

3. **Bad-set measurability.** The sets \({\mathcal B}_z\) should be shown to be measurable, and restrictions \(B\llcorner{\mathcal B}_z\) should be justified for normal currents.

4. **Bad-set Fubini estimate.** The estimate is plausible and probably true, but the proof is sketched only informally. It depends on the claim that for fixed \(x\), the set of \(z\) for which \(x\in{\mathcal B}_z\) has measure \(\lesssim R_3R_4\). This follows from the area bound for the six coordinate \(2\)-plaques, but a complete proof should spell out the area formula/measurability details.

The decisive problem is \((\mathrm{QEAT})\):
\[
 A_\Omega
 \le C\left(R_1I+\frac{I^2}{S_1q}\right)
 +\frac{C}{q}\int_Q\int_\Omega
       \Mass(C_y^0\llcorner{\mathcal B}_z)\,dy\,dz .
\]
This is not proved or cited. It is not a routine consequence of the preceding material. It is exactly the hard missing theorem.

### 7. Quotient formulation

The revision improves the previous draft by recognizing a genuine boundary-compatibility problem: if \(W_y=\partial C_y^0\), then \(f_\#W_y=f_\#Z_y\), but \(W_y-Z_y\) is only \(f\)-null, and ordinary source deformations need not preserve \(f\)-nullity.

This addresses one of the previous concerns at the conceptual level. But it introduces new technical vagueness:

- The quotient complex
  \[
  N_\ast(R,\partial R;\mathbb R)/\ker f_\#
  \]
  is not defined carefully.
- No quotient mass/filling norm is constructed.
- The bad term
  \[
  \Mass(C_y^0\llcorner{\mathcal B}_z)
  \]
  depends on a representative, not merely on a quotient class. Thus it is not clear in what precise sense \((\mathrm{QEAT})\) is “equivalent” to a quotient formulation.
- The proposed quotient or dual cochain tightening is not proved.

So the revision correctly diagnoses the earlier problem, but it does not solve it.

### 8. Conditional algebra assuming \((\mathrm{QEAT})\)

The absorption algebra is correct.

Assuming \((\mathrm{QEAT})\), the bad-set Fubini estimate gives
\[
A_\Omega
\le
C\left(R_1I+\frac{I^2}{S_1q}\right)
+
C\frac{R_3R_4}{S_3S_4}A_\Omega.
\]
If \(R_3R_4\le\kappa S_3S_4\), the last term can be absorbed for small \(\kappa\). If also
\[
I\le \delta S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4,
\]
then the two remaining terms are smaller than the lower bound for \(A_\Omega\), provided \(\delta,\kappa\) are sufficiently small. Hence
\[
I\gtrsim S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4,
\]
and weighted coarea yields the desired volume lower bound.

Thus, if \((\mathrm{QEAT})\) were proved, the hard case would be resolved. But the proof remains conditional.

---

## Which previous concerns were addressed?

Addressed or improved:

- The draft now explicitly includes the small-volume hypothesis in Guth’s isoperimetric profile.
- The first-alternative current/slicing proof is cleaner.
- The author no longer presents the old primal averaged estimate as if it were nearly proved.
- The boundary-compatibility issue for essential fillings is now acknowledged.
- The document no longer claims answer-readiness and explicitly identifies the missing theorem.

Still unresolved:

- The central missing averaged tightening theorem remains unproved.
- Measurability of selected essential fillings is not established.
- The quotient-current framework is not rigorously defined.
- The smoothing/reduction from piecewise smooth maps remains a sketch.
- Constant bookkeeping for converting \(\ge c\) estimates into the strict \(\kappa\)-dichotomy is not written out.

New issues introduced:

- The quotient formulation is not precise enough to be a theorem statement.
- The bad-set term depends on representatives and therefore is not naturally quotient-invariant.
- The phrase “Equivalently” before \((\mathrm{QEAT})\) is not justified.
- The use of real normal currents changes the coefficient category but does not explain why all relative degree and calibration arguments transfer cleanly.

---

## Final assessment

The revised draft is a useful partial reduction and is more transparent than the previous version. But it explicitly leaves an essential theorem \((\mathrm{QEAT})\) unproved. Therefore it does not solve the stated problem as a complete rigorous proof.

### Workflow compile/format feedback ###
No LaTeX compile or formatting issues detected.

### Advisory Council replies (if you requested any last turn) ###
### Council member: gpt-55-pro (models/openai/gpt-55-pro) ###
I would be cautious about the quotient-dual route as currently stated. There is a structural obstruction to getting the **source-local** bad term from **quotient** cochains.

The dual picture is: a quotient \(2\)-cochain \(\phi\) on
\[
N_\ast(R,\partial R)/\ker f_\#
\]
is the same as a functional \(\bar\phi\) on \(\operatorname{im} f_\#\). In the smooth model \(\phi=f^\ast\beta\), and the unit dual condition for essential filling is
\[
\|f^\ast d\beta\|_{\infty}\le 1.
\]
A quotient homotopy proof would need operators \(\Pi_z,K_z\) with
\[
\phi=\Pi_z\phi+\delta K_z\phi+K_z\delta\phi
\]
in the quotient, so that pairing with \(Z_y\) and using \(f_\#\partial C_y^0=f_\#Z_y\) gives
\[
\langle\phi,Z_y\rangle
=\langle K_z\delta\phi,Z_y\rangle
+\langle\delta\Pi_z\phi,C_y^0\rangle .
\]
Then the needed estimates are exactly
\[
|\langle K_z\delta\phi,Z_y\rangle|
\lesssim R_1E_y+E_y^2/S_1,
\]
and
\[
|\langle\delta\Pi_z\phi,C_y^0\rangle|
\lesssim \Mass(C_y^0\llcorner{\mathcal B}_z).
\]

The obstruction is the second estimate. If \(\Pi_z\phi\) is still a quotient cochain, then \(\delta\Pi_z\phi\) also annihilates \(\ker f_\#\). Hence it cannot be genuinely supported in the non-\(f\)-saturated source set \({\mathcal B}_z\). Smoothly, \(\delta\Pi_z\phi=f^\ast\eta\), so its pointwise support is \(f^{-1}(\operatorname{spt}\eta)\), not an arbitrary source plaque set. More invariantly: if two small oriented \(3\)-parallelepipeds \(T,T'\) have the same \(f_\#\) image, then \(T-T'\in\ker f_\#\), so any quotient cochain must take the same value on them. If \(T'\subset{\mathcal B}_z\) but \(T\cap{\mathcal B}_z=\varnothing\), a bound by \(\Mass(C\llcorner{\mathcal B}_z)\) is impossible. The exact-plaque set is generically not \(f\)-saturated; the hard-regime assumptions do not seem to repair this.

So I do not think a quotient cochain homotopy can produce the advertised bad term unless the bad norm is changed to a quotient/saturated one. The natural quotient-compatible construction would be target-side: take \(r_z:Q\setminus\{z\}\to\partial Q\), define
\[
R_z(s_1,s_2,u)=(s_1,s_2,r_z(u)),
\]
and use the de Rham homotopy
\[
K_z\omega=\int_0^1 \iota_{V_t}(\Phi_t^\ast\omega)\,dt
\]
for a homotopy \(\Phi_t\) from identity to \(R_z\). This automatically descends through \(f_\#\). The \(K_z\delta\phi\) term is plausibly where
\[
R_1E_y+\frac{E_y^2}{S_1}
\]
comes from: the \(R_1E_y\) part from primitives in the short \(x_1\)-direction after killing \(dF\)-components, and the \(E_y^2/S_1\) part from choosing/coarea-averaging an \(f_1\)-slice and filling the resulting \(G\)-seams in the \((f_1,f_2)\)-plane.

But this target-side homotopy yields a **target-saturated** singular/bad set, not the source exact-plaque \({\mathcal B}_z\). The beautiful Fubini estimate
\[
q^{-1}\int_Q \Mass(C\llcorner{\mathcal B}_z)\,dz
\lesssim (R_3R_4/S_3S_4)\Mass C
\]
is precisely source-local and appears incompatible with quotient locality.

My recommendation: either reformulate the bad term as a quotient-compatible/saturated bad norm and see whether any replacement averaging survives, or abandon quotient cochains and build primal representatives plus explicit \(f\)-null corrections. As written, QEAT asks for both quotient invariance and source-local exact-plaque support; those two requirements seem fundamentally at odds.

### Council member: opus_47_max (models/anthropic/opus_47_max) ###
Three angles to share — a structural decomposition that pins down what each term has to come from, plus the place I'd push hardest.

**1. What the two energy terms are "secretly" pricing.**

The RHS has a clean reading if you imagine constructing $C_y^0$ in two stages. Suppose you first replace $Z_y$ by a quotient-equivalent 2-cycle $W_y \in N_2(R,\partial R)$ with
$$ f_\# W_y = P_y, \qquad \Mass W_y \le C E_y \qquad (*)$$
(so $W_y - Z_y \in \ker f_\#$ and $W_y$ is "tight" against the $G$-energy). Then:

- Sweeping $W_y$ to the face $\{x_1=0\}$ in the source $x_1$-direction gives a 3-chain with $\Mass \le R_1 \Mass W_y \le C R_1 E_y$.
- Alternatively, filling the 1-cycle $\partial(\text{target slab of }W_y)$ in the *target* $G$-rectangle $[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2]$ uses the 2D rectangle profile $A^2/S_1$, yielding $\le C E_y^2/S_1$.

Taking the min over the two competitors and integrating in $y$ gives $A_\Omega \le C(R_1 I + I^2/(S_1 q))$ on $\Omega$, before the bad term. Note crucially: $S_1$ (not $R_1$) in the denominator of the second term forces the cap construction to live in *target* geometry, not source. This is the structural reason the standard source-side $A^2/R_1$ profile is too weak.

**2. The crux is (*) — and it is the same "flap" obstruction in new clothing.**

This is where I'd concentrate. The bound (*) asks the quotient mass $|[P_y]|_q := \inf\{\Mass W : f_\# W = P_y\}$ to be controlled by the $G$-Dirichlet energy of $Z_y$. From $\Dil_2\le1$ you only get the trivial $|[P_y]|_q \ge S_1S_2$, and from area-energy $S_1S_2 \le \int_{Z_y} J_2 G \le E_y/2$. The quotient formulation merely translates the geometric seam into a failure of the cochain space to be closed under the required geometric homotopy.

To get around both the boundary mismatch and the "flap obstruction" (where $Z_y$ has huge ordinary mass but small $G$-energy), you must abandon source-first deformations and build $C_y^0$ using **target-grid pullbacks**.

## Verdict

I did **not** prove `(QEAT)`, and I did **not** find a genuine counterexample to the rectangle theorem or to `(QEAT)` as a black-box estimate.  I did find a concrete finite obstruction to the natural proof strategy requested in the prompt:

> A source-local exact-plaque carrier/cochain homotopy does not descend to
> \(N_\ast(R,\partial R)/\ker f_\#\) unless it preserves \(f\)-null chains.
> Making it quotient-preserving forces nonlocal \(f\)-saturation/transfer, and
> that destroys the exact bad-mass estimate used in `(QEAT)`.

## Quotient setup

In a finite cubical model, put
\[
Q_\ast=C_\ast(R,\partial R;\mathbb R)/\ker f_\# .
\]
Equivalently \(Q_\ast\simeq \operatorname{im} f_\#\).  A source cochain descends to the quotient iff it annihilates \(\ker f_\#\).  A source chain operator \(P_z\) induces an operator on the quotient only if
\[
        P_z(\ker f_\#)\subseteq \ker f_\# .
\]
The same condition is needed in the dual construction: if \(\phi\) is a quotient cochain, then \(P_z^\ast\phi\) is again a quotient cochain only under this kernel-preservation condition.

The usual source-chain proof skeleton would use
\[
        \partial H_z(T)+H_z(\partial T)=P_zT-T
\]
and then, for an essential filling \(C_y^0\),
\[
        [Z_y]=\partial\big([P_zC_y^0]-[H_zZ_y]\big)
\]
in the quotient.  This implication requires \(P_z(\partial C_y^0-Z_y)\in\ker f_\#\).  Thus the old boundary-compatibility problem has not disappeared; it has become exactly the condition that the carrier descend to \(Q_\ast\).

## Finite obstruction

Take a relative cubical \(2\)-complex with two oriented \(2\)-cells \(A,A'\), and a target \(2\)-complex with one cell \(P\).  Define
\[
        f_\#A=P,\qquad f_\#A'=P.
\]
Then \(A-A'\in\ker f_\#\), and quotient \(2\)-cochains are precisely vectors \((x,x)\) on the ordered basis \((A,A')\).

Assume the exact-plaque bad set for a puncture \(z\) contains \(A\) but not \(A'\).  The source-local bad-cell projection is
\[
        P_{\rm bad}=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&0\end{pmatrix}.
\]
With \(f_\#=(1\;1)\) and \(N=A-A'\), the script computes
\[
        f_\#P_{\rm bad}N=-P\ne0.
\]
So \(P_{\rm bad}\) does not preserve \(\ker f_\#\).  Dually, for the quotient cochain \(\phi=(1,1)\),
\[
        P_{\rm bad}^\ast\phi=(1,0),
\]
which does not annihilate \(A-A'\).  Hence the cochain homotopy cannot be a quotient cochain homotopy.

The exact output is in `data/round34/quotient_obstruction.txt`; the key table is:

| exact-plaque mask on \((A,A')\) | descends to quotient? |
|---|---|
| \((0,0)\) | yes |
| \((1,0)\) | no |
| \((0,1)\) | no |
| \((1,1)\) | yes |

Thus a diagonal/source-local exact-plaque mask descends only when it is constant on the whole \(f\)-equivalence class.

There is a nonlocal way to preserve the quotient: send both \(A\) and \(A'\) to \(A\).  Matrix-wise,
\[
        T_{\rm bad}=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}
\]
does carry \(A-A'\) to zero.  But it violates exact bad localization: \(A'\cap{\mathcal B}_z=\emptyset\), while \(T_{\rm bad}A'=A\) has positive mass.  This is the basic tradeoff.

## PL realization of nonsaturated badness

The obstruction is not just formal.  It is realized by two disjoint cubical charts with coordinates \((u,v,s,t)\).  Let the central squares be \(A=A'=\{s=t=0\}\) in their respective charts.  On both central squares set
\[
        f(u,v,0,0)=(u,v,0,0),
\]
so both push forward to the same target square \(P\).

Extend transversely by
\[
        F_A(s,t)=(s,t),\qquad F_{A'}(s,t)=\varepsilon(s,t),
\]
where \(F=(f_3,f_4)\).  Both affine pieces have \(\operatorname{Dil}_2\le1\) in this normalized chart.  For \(z\) with \(\varepsilon\ll |z|<1\), the exact transverse coordinate plaque through \(A\) contains \(z\), while the corresponding plaque through \(A'\) does not.  Therefore \({\mathcal B}_z\) is not saturated under the relation \(A\sim A'\) induced by \(f_\#\).

If one forcibly saturates badness over \(A\sim A'\), the Fubini estimate can fail.  In the model, the exact average bad probability of \(A'\) is \(\varepsilon^2\), but the saturated bad probability is \(1\), a loss factor \(\varepsilon^{-2}\).  This is saved in `data/round34/plaque_saturation_model.txt`.

## Consequence for the requested cochain identity

The literal identity
\[
        \phi=K_z\delta\phi+\Pi_z\phi+\delta L_z\phi
\]
cannot be obtained by a source-local exact-plaque residual supported on a nonsaturated bad set.  In the two-cell relative complex above there are no \(1\)- or \(3\)-cells, so \(\delta\phi=0\) and \(\delta L_z\phi=0\).  The identity would reduce to \(\phi=\Pi_z\phi\).  But the only quotient cochain supported in the nonsaturated bad set \(\{A\}\) is zero, because quotient cochains must have equal values on \(A\) and \(A'\).

This does not disprove every possible variant of `(QEAT)`.  It rules out the standard source-local Federer--Fleming/Guth carrier route unless a new nonlocal quotient-localization mechanism is supplied.

## Minimal missing sublemma

A proof of `(QEAT)` would need something like the following, which is stronger than standard deformation theorems.

**Quotient exact-plaque carrier lemma.**  For a.e. \(z\), for each relevant slice \(Z_y\), and for each near-minimizing quotient filling \(C_y^0\), there are quotient-level operators/lifts such that, in \(Q_\ast\),
\[
        [Z_y]=\partial\big(P_z^Q[C_y^0]-H_z^Q[Z_y]\big),
\]
with
\[
        \Mass H_z^Q[Z_y]\le C\left(R_1E_y+\frac{E_y^2}{S_1}\right),
\]
and with a source lift satisfying
\[
        \Mass P_z^Q[C_y^0]\le C\,\Mass(C_y^0\llcorner{\mathcal B}_z).
\]
Equivalently in dual form, the homotopy must send quotient cochains to quotient cochains and its residual coboundary must pair only with the exact bad part of the chosen source representative \(C_y^0\).

Standard Federer--Fleming or Guth cellular carriers do not give this lemma.  They are source-local and preserve geometric skeleta/neighborhoods, not the equivalence relation generated by \(\ker f_\#\).  If modified to be quotient-invariant by saturating over \(f\)-equivalence classes, the exact-plaque Fubini estimate can be lost by the \(\varepsilon^{-2}\) example above.

Only a light secondary search was run because round 32 already did the heavy retrieval.  I found no source stating `(QEAT)` or the desired 4D rectangle \(2\)-dilation theorem. from pathlib import Path
new_answer = r"""\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{fullpage}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}

\newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}
\newtheorem{proposition}{Proposition}
\newcommand{\Dil}{\operatorname{Dil}}
\newcommand{\Vol}{\operatorname{Vol}}
\newcommand{\Mass}{\operatorname{Mass}}
\newcommand{\Fill}{\operatorname{Fill}}
\newcommand{\EFill}{\operatorname{EFill}}
\newcommand{\RR}{\mathbb R}

\begin{document}

\begin{center}
{\bf Partial proof: the first alternative and the remaining hard estimate}
\end{center}

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

I write the small universal constant in the problem as \(\kappa\), reserving
\(k\) for \(k\)-dilation.  Rectangles are oriented Euclidean products and
degree \(1\) means relative degree \(1\) on
\(H_4(R,\partial R;\mathbb Z)\to H_4(S,\partial S;\mathbb Z)\).

The current proof is complete through the first alternative and through a
conditional reduction of the hard case.  It is not a complete proof of the
stated dichotomy, because the averaged tightening estimate \((\mathrm{EAT})\)
below remains unproved.  The arguments are written for smooth transverse maps;
piecewise smooth maps may be treated by the same current-slicing argument, or
by smoothing on strata with an arbitrarily small \(C^1\)-loss and then
absorbing that loss into the universal constants.

\section*{Standard inputs}

All constants are positive and universal.  If \(\Dil_2(f)\le1\), then
\(\Dil_3(f),\Dil_4(f)\le1\): for singular values
\(\sigma_1\ge\cdots\ge\sigma_4\), the inequality
\(\sigma_1\sigma_2\le1\) implies \(\sigma_2\le1\) and
\(\sigma_1\cdots\sigma_j\le\sigma_1\sigma_2\) for \(j\ge2\).

We use Guth's map-of-pairs estimate \cite[Estimate 1]{GuthExpanding}.  For
\(n=4,k=2,j=1,l=4\) it gives
\begin{equation}\label{guth14}
        R_1^2\,\Vol(R)\ge c_0\,S_1^3S_2S_3S_4 .
\end{equation}
We also use the small-volume relative isoperimetric estimate in rectangles
\cite[Theorem 3]{GuthExpanding}: if \(Z\) is a relative \(2\)-cycle in \(R\)
with \(A=\Mass Z\le c_*R_1R_2\), then
\[
        \Fill_R(Z)\le C_*\max\{A^{3/2},A^2/R_1\}.
\]
Consequently
\begin{equation}\label{profile-inverse}
\Fill_R(Z)\ge L\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Mass Z\ge c\min\{R_1R_2,L^{2/3},(R_1L)^{1/2}\},
\end{equation}
and, after decreasing \(c_*\),
\begin{equation}\label{iso}
        \Mass Z<c_*R_1R_2\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
        \Fill_R(Z)\le C R_1R_2^2 .
\end{equation}

\section*{The first alternative}

Let \(Q\) be the concentric subrectangle of the \((x_3,x_4)\)-face of \(S\)
with side lengths \(S_3/2,S_4/2\), and set
\[
        P_y=[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2]\times\{y\},\qquad y\in Q.
\]

\begin{lemma}[central target plane]\label{central}
Every relative \(3\)-chain in \(S\) with boundary \(P_y\), \(y\in Q\), has
mass at least \(cS_1S_2S_3\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Choose a \(1\)-Lipschitz function \(\psi\) on the
\((x_3,x_4)\)-rectangle which vanishes on its boundary and satisfies
\(\psi\ge cS_3\) on \(Q\).  Put \(\beta=\psi\,dx_1\wedge dx_2\).  Then
\(d\beta\) has comass at most \(1\), and Stokes' theorem gives
\[
\Mass Y\ge\left|\int_Yd\beta\right|
 =\left|\int_{P_y}\beta\right|\ge cS_1S_2S_3 .
\]
The relative boundary terms vanish because \(dx_1\wedge dx_2=0\) on the
\(x_1,x_2\)-faces and \(\psi=0\) on the \(x_3,x_4\)-faces.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}\label{firstalt}
There is \(c_2>0\) such that, under the hypotheses of the problem, if
\[
        R_1R_2^2\le c_2S_1S_2S_3 ,
\]
then \(R_3R_4\ge c_2S_3S_4\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(T=[[R]]\) be the fundamental relative current and put \(F=(f_3,f_4)\).
Since \(f\) has relative degree \(1\), \(f_\#T=[[S]]\) as a relative
\(4\)-current.  For a.e. \(y\in Q\), slicing naturality gives
\[
        f_\#\langle T,F,y\rangle
        =\langle [[S]],(x_3,x_4),y\rangle=\pm P_y .
\]
Write \(Z_y=\langle T,F,y\rangle\).  Any relative filling of \(Z_y\) in \(R\)
pushes forward, using \(\Dil_3(f)\le1\), to a filling of \(P_y\) in \(S\).
Lemma \ref{central} therefore gives
\[
        \Fill_R(Z_y)\ge cS_1S_2S_3 .
\]
If \(c_2\) is sufficiently small, \eqref{iso} forces
\(\Mass Z_y\ge c_*R_1R_2\).  Since \(J_2F\le\Dil_2(f)\le1\), the coarea
formula yields
\[
 \Vol(R)\ge\int_Q\Mass Z_y\,dy
        \ge cR_1R_2S_3S_4 .
\]
Cancelling \(R_1R_2\) proves the proposition.
\end{proof}

\section*{The unconditional partial hard-case bound}

Assume \(R_1=\alpha S_1\le S_1\) and that the first conclusion fails with a
small constant.  Proposition \ref{firstalt} gives
\begin{equation}\label{r12large}
        R_1R_2^2\ge cS_1S_2S_3 .
\end{equation}
For the slices above, \eqref{profile-inverse} with
\(L=cS_1S_2S_3\) gives
\[
\Mass Z_y\ge c\min\{R_1R_2,(S_1S_2S_3)^{2/3},
                         (R_1S_1S_2S_3)^{1/2}\}.
\]
Using \eqref{r12large} and \(R_1=\alpha S_1\), every term in the minimum is
at least \(c\alpha^{1/2}S_1(S_2S_3)^{1/2}\).  Hence
\begin{equation}\label{slicevol}
        \Vol(R)\ge
        c\alpha^{1/2}S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4 .
\end{equation}
On the other hand, \eqref{guth14} gives
\begin{equation}\label{monovol}
        \Vol(R)\ge
        c\alpha^{-2}S_1S_2S_3S_4 .
\end{equation}
Optimizing between \eqref{slicevol} and \eqref{monovol} gives
\[
        \Vol(R)\ge cS_1S_2^{3/5}S_3^{7/5}S_4 .
\]
Thus the remaining gap is exactly the high-aspect factor
\((S_3/S_2)^{1/10}\).

\section*{A sufficient averaged hard-case estimate}

Let \(G=(f_1,f_2)\), keep \(F=(f_3,f_4)\), and define
\[
E_y=\int_{Z_y}\|dG|_{TZ_y}\|_{HS}^2\,d\|Z_y\|,\qquad
I=\int_QE_y\,dy,\qquad q=|Q|.
\]
At a regular point of \(F\), let \(K=\ker dF\), and let \(a\ge b\) be the
singular values of \(dF|_{K^\perp}\); then \(ab=J_2F\).  For any unit
\(w\in K\), the mixed two-vector \(df(w)\wedge df(v)\), where \(v\) is a
unit vector with \(|dF(v)|=a\), has a component of norm
\(|dG(w)|a\).  Since \(\Dil_2(f)\le1\), \(|dG(w)|a\le1\), and hence
\(|dG(w)|^2J_2F\le b/a\le1\).  Summing over an orthonormal basis of \(K\)
gives
\[
        \|dG|_{\ker dF}\|_{HS}^2J_2F\le2 .
\]
By coarea,
\begin{equation}\label{Icoarea}
        I\le C\Vol(R).
\end{equation}
Let \(\Omega=\{y\in Q:E_y\le4I/q\}\), so \(|\Omega|\ge3q/4\).

For real normal currents define
\[
\EFill_f(y)=\inf\{\Mass C:\ C\in N_3(R,\partial R;\mathbb R),\
        f_\#(\partial C)=\pm P_y\}.
\]
This class is nonempty by filling the actual slice \(Z_y\).  If \(C\) is
admissible, then \(f_\#C\) fills \(P_y\) and \(\Mass(f_\#C)\le\Mass C\);
Lemma \ref{central} gives
\begin{equation}\label{EFilllower}
        \EFill_f(y)\ge cS_1S_2S_3 .
\end{equation}
Therefore
\begin{equation}\label{AElower}
A_\Omega:=\int_\Omega\EFill_f(y)\,dy
        \ge cS_1S_2S_3\,q
        \ge cS_1S_2S_3^2S_4 .
\end{equation}

For \(z\in Q\), let \({\mathcal B}_z\) be the set of \(x\in R\) such that
\(z\) belongs to the \(F\)-image of at least one coordinate \(2\)-plaque
through \(x\).  For fixed \(x\), the set of such \(z\)'s has measure at most
\(CR_3R_4\), because there are six plaques, each has area at most \(R_3R_4\),
and \(J_2F\le1\) on each plaque.  Hence, for every normal \(3\)-current \(B\),
\begin{equation}\label{badfubini}
 \frac1q\int_Q\Mass(B\llcorner{\mathcal B}_z)\,dz
 \le C\frac{R_3R_4}{S_3S_4}\Mass B .
\end{equation}

The following estimate is sufficient for the desired theorem.  Take measurable
near-minimizers \(C_y^0\) with
\(\Mass C_y^0\le2\EFill_f(y)\); standard measurable selection in the
separable flat topology, or equivalently outer integration with
\(\varepsilon\)-minimizers, is enough for this formal step.  One needs
\begin{equation}\tag{EAT}\label{EAT}
 A_\Omega
 \le C\left(R_1I+\frac{I^2}{S_1q}\right)
 +\frac{C}{q}\int_Q\int_\Omega
       \Mass(C_y^0\llcorner{\mathcal B}_z)\,dy\,dz .
\end{equation}

Assuming \eqref{EAT}, \eqref{badfubini} and
\(\Mass C_y^0\le2\EFill_f(y)\) give
\[
A_\Omega\le C\left(R_1I+\frac{I^2}{S_1q}\right)
  +C\frac{R_3R_4}{S_3S_4}A_\Omega .
\]
If \(R_3R_4\le\kappa S_3S_4\), the last term is absorbed by taking
\(\kappa\) small.  If also \(R_1\le\kappa S_1\) and
\(I\le\delta S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4\), then, since \(q\sim S_3S_4\),
\[
R_1I\le C\kappa\delta S_1S_2S_3^2S_4,\qquad
\frac{I^2}{S_1q}\le C\delta^2S_1S_2S_3^2S_4 .
\]
For small \(\delta,\kappa\) this contradicts \eqref{AElower}.  Thus
\(I\ge cS_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4\), and \eqref{Icoarea} gives the desired
second alternative.

\section*{Why the remaining estimate is non-routine}

The estimate \eqref{EAT} cannot be obtained by a standard source-local
cellular deformation in the quotient by \(f\)-null chains without a new idea.
The obstruction is already visible in a two-cell model.  Let \(A,A'\) be two
oriented \(2\)-cells with \(f_\#A=f_\#A'=P\).  Then \(N=A-A'\in\ker f_\#\).
If an exact-plaque bad set contains \(A\) but not \(A'\), the source-local
projection \(P_{\rm bad}\) with \(P_{\rm bad}A=A\), \(P_{\rm bad}A'=0\) gives
\(f_\#P_{\rm bad}N=P\ne0\).  Dually, a quotient cochain has equal values on
\(A\) and \(A'\), but restricting it to the nonsaturated bad set destroys that
property.  Thus exact source localization and quotient invariance are in
tension.

This does not disprove \eqref{EAT}; it identifies what is missing.  A proof
would need an energy-sensitive, null-respecting carrier or a dual target-side
cochain homotopy whose residual term is nevertheless controlled by the
unsaturated exact-plaque mass in \eqref{badfubini}.

\section*{Remaining open issues}

The document is therefore a conditional reduction, not a complete solution.

\begin{enumerate}
\item The essential mathematical gap is \eqref{EAT}, an averaged tightening
estimate for the degree-carrying part of the slices \(Z_y\).  It is used only
in the last section, but it is indispensable for the high-aspect hard case.

\item The first alternative, the single-slice bound, the weighted coarea
estimate, the essential filling lower bound, the exact-plaque Fubini estimate,
and the absorption algebra are proved above.

\item Standard approaches tried so far do not close the gap: monomial
degree estimates give only \eqref{guth14}; ordinary source deformations create
uncontrolled \(f\)-null boundary errors; quotient cochains are not compatible
with nonsaturated exact-plaque localization as shown above.

\item A complete proof would require either a proof of \eqref{EAT}, an
equivalent dual formulation using only target-pullback cochains, or a different
hard-regime argument exploiting the full two-parameter family of slices and the
ambient mixed \(2\)-Jacobian bounds.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{thebibliography}{9}

\bibitem{GuthExpanding}
L. Guth, \emph{Area-expanding embeddings of rectangles}, arXiv:0710.0403.

\bibitem{GuthThesis}
L. Guth, \emph{Area-contracting maps between rectangles}, Ph.D. thesis,
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, 2005.

\bibitem{GuthDirectional}
L. Guth, \emph{Directional isoperimetric inequalities and rational homotopy
invariants}, arXiv:0802.3549.

\end{thebibliography}

\end{document}
